# Proof Map

| Claim | Closest proof type | Best sources |
|---|---|---|
| Social proof changes behaviour | Randomized field experiment | Muchnik, Aral & Taylor 2013 |
| Visible popularity changes cultural markets | Controlled artificial market | Salganik, Dodds & Watts 2006 |
| Early arbitrary success can cause more success | Randomized field experiments | van de Rijt et al. 2014 |
| Bots can help early amplification | Large-scale observational network analysis | Shao et al. 2018 |
| Coordinated attention can be detected mathematically | Network construction + repeated co-action model | Rogers & Righetti 2025; Graham et al. 2024 |
| Recommenders can amplify feedback and popularity | Simulation and recommender-system surveys | Chaney et al. 2018; Klimashevskaia et al. 2024; Zoralioglu & Yalcin 2026 |
| Fake reviews alter demand and welfare | Incentive-compatible experiment | Akesson et al. 2023 |
| Profile injection can manipulate recommenders | Recommender security literature | Yang & Niu 2021; Zhang et al. 2024 |
| Link farms can manipulate link-ranking systems | Web-spam graph research | Wu & Davison 2005 |
| Threshold effects can cause cascades | Mathematical sociology/network models | Granovetter 1978; Watts 2002 |

## Paper-ready conclusion

The strongest defensible argument is not "every manufactured campaign always works." The proof is conditional:

> When allocation depends on manipulable proxy signals, and the manipulated signal crosses a ranking, belief, threshold, or payment boundary, manufactured traction can change real outcomes.
